questions.stem: An ecological niche is best described as:

options.text: The role of a species in an ecosystem
The place where a species lives

questions.explanation: An ecological niche is the role of a species in its ecosystem, including its biotic and abiotic interactions.

questions.stem: A species' niche includes interactions that influence its:
questions.explanation: The niche includes biotic and abiotic interactions that influence a species' growth, survival and reproduction, including how it obtains food.

questions.scenario: An organism cannot survive in the presence of oxygen.
questions.stem: It is best described as an:
questions.explanation: Obligate anaerobes cannot tolerate oxygen; obligate aerobes require it; facultative anaerobes can live with or without it.

questions.scenario: An organism can live with or without oxygen.
questions.stem: It is a:
questions.explanation: Facultative anaerobes can survive with or without oxygen, unlike the obligate types.

questions.scenario: An organism requires oxygen to survive.
questions.stem: It is an:
questions.explanation: Obligate aerobes require oxygen for their metabolism.

questions.stem: Photosynthesis is the mode of nutrition in plants, algae and:
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis occurs in plants, algae and several groups of photosynthetic prokaryotes.

questions.stem: In holozoic nutrition, food is:

options.text: Ingested, digested internally, absorbed and assimilated
Made from carbon dioxide and water

questions.explanation: In holozoic nutrition, seen in animals, food is ingested, digested internally, absorbed and assimilated.

questions.stem: All animals obtain their food by which broad mode of nutrition?
questions.explanation: All animals are heterotrophic, obtaining organic food from other organisms; holozoic nutrition is one form of this.

questions.scenario: Euglena can photosynthesize but also ingest food.
questions.stem: This mode of nutrition is:
questions.explanation: Mixotrophs such as Euglena can be both autotrophic and heterotrophic.

questions.stem: A mixotroph that can switch between autotrophy and heterotrophy as needed is:
questions.explanation: Some mixotrophs are obligate and others are facultative, able to switch modes as conditions require.

questions.scenario: Some fungi and bacteria feed on dead organic matter by secreting enzymes onto it.
questions.stem: This mode of nutrition is:
questions.explanation: Saprotrophic organisms digest dead matter externally and absorb the products; they are decomposers.

questions.stem: Saprotrophic fungi and bacteria are also known as:
questions.explanation: Saprotrophs feeding on dead organic matter are referred to as decomposers.

questions.stem: Archaea are described as being metabolically:
questions.explanation: Archaea, one of the three domains, are metabolically very diverse in how they obtain energy.

questions.stem: Which energy source can archaea use for ATP production?

options.text: Light, oxidation of inorganic chemicals, or oxidation of carbon compounds
None; archaea do not make ATP

questions.explanation: Archaea use light, oxidation of inorganic chemicals, or oxidation of carbon compounds to provide energy for ATP production.

questions.scenario: A skull has flat, broad molars and few sharp teeth.
questions.stem: This dentition suggests a diet that is largely:
questions.explanation: Broad, flat molars are adapted for grinding plant material, suggesting a herbivorous diet.

questions.scenario: Scientists infer the diet of an extinct hominid from its teeth.
questions.stem: This is possible because theories relating dentition to diet allow:

options.text: Deductions about the diet of extinct organisms
Direct observation of the extinct animal feeding
The genome to be read from teeth

questions.explanation: Theories linking dentition to diet in living mammals allow the diet of extinct organisms to be deduced from their teeth.

questions.scenario: A plant produces toxic compounds in its leaves.
questions.stem: This is an adaptation for:
questions.explanation: Producing toxic secondary compounds is a way plants resist herbivory; thorns are a physical example.

questions.scenario: Some insects have piercing and chewing mouthparts.
questions.stem: These are adaptations for:
questions.explanation: Piercing and chewing mouthparts are herbivore adaptations for feeding on plant material such as leaves.

questions.scenario: An animal has enzymes that break down a plant toxin.
questions.stem: This is an example of:

options.text: A metabolic adaptation for detoxifying plant defences

questions.explanation: Some herbivores have metabolic adaptations for detoxifying the toxic secondary compounds produced by plants.

questions.scenario: A predator has sharp claws and excellent night vision.
questions.stem: These are adaptations for:
questions.explanation: Predators have chemical, physical and behavioural adaptations for finding, catching and killing prey.

questions.scenario: A prey animal freezes and blends into its background when a predator is near.
questions.stem: This is an example of a:
questions.explanation: Camouflage and freezing are behavioural and physical adaptations that help prey resist predation.

questions.scenario: A liana climbs up tree trunks to reach sunlight in a forest.
questions.stem: This is an adaptation of plant form for:
questions.explanation: Lianas, like canopy trees and epiphytes, are adaptations of plant form for harvesting light in forests.

questions.scenario: An epiphyte grows on the branches of a tall tree.
questions.stem: How does this help the epiphyte?

options.text: It reaches light higher in the forest
It fixes nitrogen for the tree

questions.explanation: Epiphytes grow on tree branches to reach light, one of several strategies plants use to harvest light in forests.

questions.stem: The fundamental niche of a species is:

options.text: Its potential niche based on adaptations and tolerance limits
Its actual niche when competing with others

questions.explanation: The fundamental niche is a species' potential niche given its adaptations and tolerance limits, in the absence of competition.

questions.stem: The realized niche of a species is:

options.text: The actual niche it occupies when competing with other species
Its full potential niche with no competition

questions.explanation: The realized niche is the actual extent of the niche when the species is in competition with others, usually smaller than the fundamental niche.

questions.scenario: Two species compete for exactly the same resources in the same place.
questions.stem: According to the competitive exclusion principle, a likely outcome is:

options.text: One species is eliminated or both are restricted to part of their niche
Both occupy the identical niche indefinitely

questions.explanation: Competitive exclusion means two species cannot occupy the same niche; one is eliminated or both are restricted to part of their fundamental niche.

questions.stem: The competitive exclusion principle implies that ecological niches are:
questions.explanation: Because two species cannot coexist in exactly the same niche, each species' niche is effectively unique.

questions.stem: How a species obtains its food is part of its:
questions.explanation: The niche includes how a species obtains food, among its biotic and abiotic interactions.

questions.stem: The three groups obligate anaerobe, facultative anaerobe and obligate aerobe differ in their:
questions.explanation: These groups are distinguished by their tolerance of the presence or absence of oxygen in the environment.

questions.stem: Organisms that make their own food from inorganic sources by photosynthesis are:
questions.explanation: Photosynthetic organisms are autotrophs, making organic food from inorganic sources using light.

questions.stem: In saprotrophic nutrition, digestion occurs:
questions.explanation: Saprotrophs secrete enzymes to digest dead matter externally, then absorb the products; this contrasts with holozoic nutrition.

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly describes holozoic nutrition?
questions.explanation: Holozoic nutrition proceeds by ingestion, internal digestion, absorption and assimilation.

questions.stem: Archaea are one of the three:
questions.explanation: Archaea are one of the three domains of life (with Bacteria and Eukaryota) and are metabolically very diverse.

questions.scenario: The family Hominidae includes omnivorous and herbivorous members.
questions.stem: Studying their dentition allows inferences about their:
questions.explanation: Dentition is related to diet, so examining teeth in Hominidae allows diet to be inferred.

questions.scenario: A plant has sharp thorns along its stems.
questions.stem: These thorns are a:
questions.explanation: Thorns are a physical structure by which plants resist herbivory.

questions.scenario: A prey species produces a foul-tasting chemical.
questions.stem: This is an example of a:
questions.explanation: Producing distasteful chemicals is a chemical adaptation by which prey resist predation.

questions.scenario: A shrub grows well in the low light of the forest floor.
questions.stem: It is best described as:
questions.explanation: Shade-tolerant shrubs and herbs are adapted to harvest the limited light available on the forest floor.

questions.scenario: A species could potentially live across a wide range of conditions, but competition restricts it to a narrower range.
questions.stem: The narrower range it actually occupies is its:
questions.explanation: Competition restricts a species to its realized niche, which is smaller than its fundamental niche.

questions.scenario: Two competing species end up using slightly different parts of a habitat.
questions.stem: This outcome of competition is:

options.text: Restriction of both to part of their fundamental niche

questions.explanation: Competition may restrict both species to parts of their fundamental niche, a possible outcome under competitive exclusion.

questions.stem: Which best distinguishes a niche from a habitat?

options.text: A niche is a role; a habitat is a place
A niche is a place; a habitat is a role

questions.explanation: A niche is the role a species plays; a habitat is the place where it lives.

questions.scenario: Many mixotrophic species are part of oceanic plankton.
questions.stem: This shows that mixotrophy is:
questions.explanation: Besides the freshwater example Euglena, many oceanic plankton are mixotrophic, showing the mode is widespread.

questions.stem: Why are decomposers important in ecosystems?

options.text: They break down dead organic matter, recycling nutrients

questions.explanation: Saprotrophic decomposers break down dead matter externally, releasing nutrients back into the ecosystem.

questions.scenario: A skull has large, sharp canine teeth.
questions.stem: This dentition suggests a diet including:
questions.explanation: Large sharp canines are associated with tearing meat, indicating a carnivorous or omnivorous diet.

questions.stem: Plant adaptations to resist herbivory include:

options.text: Physical structures like thorns and toxic secondary compounds

questions.explanation: Plants resist herbivory with physical structures such as thorns and by producing toxic secondary compounds.

questions.stem: Adaptations of predators and prey can be:
questions.explanation: Both predators and prey show chemical, physical and behavioural adaptations.

questions.scenario: A tall tree grows straight up to reach the forest canopy.
questions.stem: This strategy for harvesting light is used by:
questions.explanation: Reaching the canopy is one of several strategies (with lianas, epiphytes, shade-tolerant plants) for harvesting light in forests.

questions.stem: The realized niche is usually smaller than the fundamental niche because of:
questions.explanation: Competition restricts a species to its realized niche, narrower than its potential fundamental niche.

questions.stem: Which is a possible outcome when two species compete for the same niche?

options.text: Both expand into a larger identical niche

questions.explanation: Competitive exclusion can lead to one species being eliminated or both being restricted to part of their fundamental niche.

questions.stem: Which group is NOT capable of photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis occurs in plants, algae and some prokaryotes; animals are heterotrophic and do not photosynthesize.

questions.stem: Which factors are included in a species' ecological niche?
questions.explanation: A niche includes the biotic and abiotic interactions affecting a species' growth, survival and reproduction.
